objections.objection: S1 presumes teacher supply and readiness for multigrade pedagogy will remain adequate without systemic training reform. This directly contradicts the dossier and synthesis evidence of acute rural staffing problems (16,000 unfilled posts cited in the synthesis, an ageing teacher workforce, and 2-3x higher unqualified-teacher rates in disadvantaged rural schools). The scenario offers no credible pathway or numbers showing how existing shortages would be resolved while relying on status-quo training, making the core viability claim unsupported by the evidence.
objections.suggested_revision: Replace the implicit ‘no systemic reform’ assumption with explicit, costed teacher-capacity conditions required for S1 to be viable: (a) a multiyear teacher-recruitment target (e.g., X new rural-qualified teachers/year) and associated incentive package; (b) a defined multigrade in-service training rollout (content, budget, timeline) with fidelity metrics; and (c) preconditions/triggers (teacher-vacancy and qualification thresholds) that must be met in a settlement before switching to multigrade rather than using it as a stopgap. Include monitoring indicators and a contingency plan if thresholds are not met (e.g., temporary funded traveling-teacher pools or planned consolidation).

objections.objection: S3 assumes the central government would permit reallocation of maintainer authority or create a new legal category enabling inter-municipal/association-run schools. This is contradicted by the synthesis and scenario text documenting post-2013 centralization and recent coercive asset-transfer laws (2023) that materially reduce municipal discretion. The scenario treats a politically and legally non-trivial reversal as an implementation-detail rather than a core barrier.
objections.suggested_revision: Make the legal/political barrier an explicit, binding precondition: (a) scope a specific legal amendment package needed (statute sections to change, parliamentary steps), with timeline and likelihood assessment; (b) design a pilot-authorisation pathway (time-limited ministerial waivers for 2–3 regions) to test association-run models without full legislative change; and (c) include protective clauses (e.g., statutory asset-protection and non-revocation guarantees for pilot schools) and an advocacy/stakeholder-engagement strategy with concrete milestones before national scale-up is assumed feasible.

objections.objection: S4 assumes TEIP-style targeted support and statutory transport guarantees will meaningfully offset the equity harms of closure. That assumption overstates what the cited evidence supports: the dossier itself notes Portugal’s TEIP reduced dropout/failure but ‘‘failed over 15 years to close the underlying performance gap’’. Thus the scenario treats a partial mitigation (dropout reduction) as if it were full compensation for closure harms, which the evidence does not support.
objections.suggested_revision: Recast TEIP/transport as partial mitigants and require pre-closure evidence-of-readiness: (a) mandate a pre-closure quality audit of receiving schools with explicit pass/fail criteria (teacher qualifications, class sizes, remedial capacity) that must be satisfied before any closure; (b) pilot a Hungarian TEIP-adaptation with matched controls and at least 5-year evaluation before large-scale use as a compensatory justification; and (c) require legally funded transport guarantees (route-safety, travel-time caps, costed budgets) to be in place and independently verified prior to closure decisions.

objections.objection: S5 assumes a non-binding, advisory national registry and threshold framework will be used neutrally rather than selectively invoked to justify pre-determined closure decisions. The synthesis and political-risk discussion show selective invocation is a realistic and documented risk under current governance incentives (tankerületi discretion, recent centralizing laws), so treating neutrality as the baseline is unsupported.
objections.suggested_revision: Change from assuming neutrality to designing safeguards: (a) commit to making at least some registry outputs and threshold checks binding (e.g., automatic review triggers or moratoria where thresholds are exceeded); (b) create an independent oversight/audit body with statutory powers to review closure decisions against the registry and publish findings; and (c) designate and ring-fence registry funding in law to reduce the risk of underfunding or political de-prioritization.
